key_facts: Gerald Ross Pizzuto, Jr., a death-row inmate in Idaho, filed a complaint under 42 U.S.C § 1983 alleging that his execution would violate the Eighth Amendment’s prohibition against cruel and unusual punishment.
Idaho obtained execution protocol drugs for use in the execution of another death-row inmate.
Plaintiff sought discovery about the origin, manufacturing, and acquisition of these drugs.
Idaho refused to disclose the identity of the execution drug supplier, citing the state’s execution secrecy statute.
The district court found the information relevant and not protected by privilege, and did not find an undue burden in the disclosure.
legal_issues: Whether the court has jurisdiction to review the district court’s interlocutory discovery order under the collateral order doctrine.
Whether the district court abused its discretion in ordering the disclosure of the execution drug supplier’s identity.
Whether the disclosure of the execution drug supplier’s identity imposes an undue burden on the state.
decision: The panel affirmed the district court’s order granting discovery about the origin, manufacturing, and acquisition of the execution protocol drugs. The court found that the order fell into the narrow class of cases satisfying the collateral order doctrine, and that the district court did not abuse its discretion in ordering the disclosure.
reasoning_summary: The panel held that the district court did not abuse its discretion in ordering the disclosure of the execution drug supplier’s identity. The court found that the information was relevant to Pizzuto’s claims and that Idaho failed to show how the disclosure would impose an undue burden. The panel also held that the court had jurisdiction to review the district court’s interlocutory discovery order under the collateral order doctrine.
